headline: Modest Mouse Frontman Isaac Brock Born, 1975
body:
The great Missouri, that untamed serpent of the plains, had long defied the ambitions of man in eastern Montana. For generations, its broad, often treacherous currents had carved a chasm between communities, isolating the fertile lands of Roosevelt and McCone counties. Travel meant ferry crossings, often perilous, or circuitous detours that bled time and resource from the hardworking farmers and ranchers. But the spirit of progress, that relentless engine of civilization, would not be gainsaid. After years of tireless lobbying by men of vision, chief among them Wolf Point businessman William Young, the Montana Highway Commission and the Bureau of Public Roads finally gave their assent in 1927. The challenge was laid, and the Missouri Valley Bridge and Iron Company of Leavenworth, Kansas, a firm well-versed in the taming of rivers, took up the gauntlet in 1929.

What followed was a symphony of steel and sweat, a testament to human ingenuity against the raw power of nature. A veritable town sprang up on the north bank, a temporary encampment humming with the industry of men and machines. There were powerhouses to drive the rivets, workshops to shape the girders, offices to manage the immense undertaking, and even a dance hall, a bunkhouse, and cottages for the families who followed the work. For nearly two years, this construction site, far from any established metropolis, became the most popular attraction in northeastern Montana, a magnet for the curious and the hopeful. They watched as the colossal framework rose, piece by painstaking piece, a skeletal leviathan stretching across the formidable river.

And then, the day arrived. The ninth of July, in the year of our Lord 1930. The Wolf Point Bridge, a marvel of modern engineering, stood complete. The air thrummed with anticipation, a palpable excitement that drew a multitude. Over fifteen thousand souls, a throng from every corner of Montana and even from the distant reaches of Canada, converged upon Wolf Point. It was a spectacle of grand proportions, a celebration befitting such a monumental achievement. Speeches were delivered, their words echoing the pride and foresight that had brought this structure into being. Bands played stirring tunes, their brass and drums adding to the festive din. A float, adorned with symbols of prosperity and progress, moved through the jubilant crowd. Cowboys, their chaps dusty and their faces weathered, added a touch of frontier authenticity to the proceedings. And as the sun climbed high, a daylight fireworks show erupted, painting transient bursts of color against the vast Montana sky.

But beyond the fanfare and the spectacle, there was a deeper, more profound significance. Tribal elders from the Fort Peck Reservation, their wisdom rooted in generations of observing the river's moods, offered their blessings, acknowledging the bridge's place in the ancient landscape. This was no mere crossing; it was a lifeline. It promised safer, easier passage, a direct conduit for goods and people, linking isolated communities to the vital Great Northern rail line. The bridge, with its 1,074 feet of length and 1,150 tons of steel, boasted a 400-foot span, making it the longest and most massive through truss in the entire state of Montana. It was, as one journalist of the era eloquently put it, a structure that,
